1:
"And he arose from thence, and cometh into the coasts of Judaea by the farther side of Jordan: and the people resort unto him again; and, as he was wont, he taught them again." The witnesses note the transition to the Judaean (Peraean) ministry and the renewed teaching of the crowds. Bede: "Up to this time, Mark hath related what Our Lord said and did in Galilee; here he begins to relate what He did, taught, or suffered in Judaea, and first indeed across the Jordan on the east." Robertson sets the chronology: "Jesus has begun his last journey to Jerusalem going north through Samaria, Galilee, across the Jordan into Perea, and back into Judea near Jericho... A great deal has intervened between the events at the close of Mark 9 and those in the beginning of Mark 10" (much of Luke and John 7-11). Lightfoot and Ellicott note the long interval the other Gospels fill.

The Antiochene and Latin fathers contrast the crowds with the Pharisees. Bede: "the former meet together, in order to be taught... the latter come to Him, to try to deceive their Saviour by tempting Him." Theophylact: Christ "enters the region of Judaea, which the envy of the Jews had often caused Him to leave, because His Passion was to take place there," yet went "to the confines of Judaea, that He might do good to the multitudes." On the divorce question, he notes the Pharisees "proposed to Him a question, which had on either side a precipice," but "Christ, being Very Wisdom, in answering their question, avoids their snares."

The puritan Henry (covering vv.1-12): "Our Lord Jesus was an itinerant Preacher... the whole land of Canaan was his parish." Wherever he was, "they came to him again... and, as he was wont, he taught them again. Note, Even those whom Christ hath taught, have need to be taught again." On the divorce question: "This was a good question, if it had been well put... but they proposed it, tempting him." On Moses' permission: "the reason why Moses, in his law, permitted divorce, was such, as that they ought not to make use of that permission; for it was only for the hardness of their hearts."

The witnesses agree: Christ, beginning his last journey toward Jerusalem, came into the Judaean territory beyond Jordan, where the crowds again resorted to him and he taught them as was his constant practice. The chapter opens his Peraean ministry, in which teaching and healing went together, and in which the Pharisees, envying his progress, would seek to ensnare him with the divorce question (v.2). The renewed teaching of even those already taught, the fathers note, breeds not weariness but hunger — for the honied words of wisdom yield ever fresh savour to those who love her.
2:
"And the Pharisees came to him, and asked him, Is it lawful for a man to put away his wife? tempting him." The witnesses note the malicious intent behind the question and its trap. Gill: the Pharisees came "not to be instructed by him, but to ensnare him." Robertson: "As soon as Jesus appears in Galilee the Pharisees attack him again... their motive was evil. They had once involved the Baptist with Herod Antipas and Herodias on this subject. They may have some such hopes about Jesus, or their purpose may have been to see if Jesus will be stricter than Moses taught."

The question was a snare on both sides. Theophylact (under v.1) had explained: "they proposed to Him a question, which had on either side a precipice, so that whether He said that it was lawful for a man to put away his wife, or that it was not lawful, they might accuse Him." If he forbade divorce, he would seem to contradict Moses; if he allowed it freely, he would seem lax — and either way they hoped to discredit him or, perhaps, to entangle him with Herod (who had married his brother's wife), as the Baptist had been entangled to his death.

The puritan Henry (under v.1) noted the lesson for ministers: "Is it lawful for a man to put away his wife? This was a good question, if it had been well put, and with a humble desire to know the mind of God in this matter; but they proposed it, tempting him, seeking an occasion against him... Ministers must stand upon their guard, lest, under pretence of being advised with, they be ensnared." The very question that might have been asked in sincerity was here a trap.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Pharisees' question about divorce was no honest inquiry but a snare, designed to discredit Christ whichever way he answered — and perhaps to entangle him with Herod as they had entangled John. The malice of the questioners stands in contrast to the crowds who came to be taught (v.1). Christ, the very Wisdom of God, would not be caught, but would answer by turning them to the law of Moses (v.3) and then beyond it to the creation ordinance (vv.6-9). The verse exposes the persistent hostility of the Pharisees, who, envying Christ's influence, sought every occasion to destroy him; and it warns Christ's servants to be wary of questions put not to learn but to ensnare. The trap laid in malice becomes the occasion of Christ's clearest teaching on marriage.
3:
"And he answered and said unto them, What did Moses command you?" The brief verse records Christ's wise counter-question, turning the Pharisees back to the law. Gill: he answered "very prudently and wisely." Robertson: "Jesus at once brought up the issue concerning the teaching of Moses (Deuteronomy 24:1). But Jesus goes back beyond this concession here allowed by Moses to the ideal state commanded in Genesis 1:27."

Christ's counter-question the witnesses read as both prudent and instructive. Theophylact (under v.1) explained the strategy: "being asked, whether it is lawful, he does not immediately reply, it is not lawful, lest they should raise an outcry, but He first wished them to answer Him as to the sentence of the law, that they by their answer might furnish Him with what it was right to say." By making them state Moses' provision first, Christ drew from their own mouths the ground on which he would build his answer.

The puritan Henry (under v.1) noted the respect for Moses: Christ asked this "to testify his respect to the law of Moses, and to show that he came not to destroy it; and to engage them to a universal impartial respect for Moses's writings and to compare one part of them with another." Far from setting himself against Moses, Christ would show that Moses' own writings, rightly understood, taught the indissolubility of marriage (vv.6-9), the permission of divorce being only a concession to hardness of heart (v.5).

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which records Christ's counter-question. The witnesses agree: Christ, with prudent wisdom, met the Pharisees' trap by turning them back to Moses — "What did Moses command you?" — drawing from their own answer the ground for his reply, and showing his respect for the law he came not to destroy but to fulfil. The counter-question avoided their snare, for it neither denied Moses' provision nor endorsed their lax practice, but prepared to set the temporary permission (Deuteronomy 24:1) against the abiding creation ordinance (Genesis 1-2). By making them cite Moses, Christ would show that Moses himself, in the deeper sense, forbade what the permission only tolerated. (The Jamieson-Fausset-Brown fragment here, on the seed growing secretly, belongs to Mark 4, not this verse.) The verse displays the wisdom of Christ, who turns the questioner's own authority into the instrument of the answer.
4:
"And they said, Moses suffered to write a bill of divorcement, and to put her away." The witnesses note the Pharisees' answer and the precise meaning of Moses' provision. Robertson: "The word for bill is a diminutive and means little book... Wycliff has it here a libel of forsaking. This same point the Pharisees raise in Matthew 19:7, showing probably that they held to the liberal view of Hillel, easy divorce for almost any cause. That was the popular view as now... Jesus expounds the purpose of marriage (Genesis 2:24) and takes the stricter view of divorce, that of the school of Shammai." Barnes cross-references Deuteronomy 24:1.

The Pharisees' answer rightly states the fact: Moses "suffered" — permitted — a bill of divorcement (Deuteronomy 24:1). The witnesses note the careful word. Henry (under v.1): "They own that Moses only suffered, or permitted, a man to write his wife a bill of divorce, and to put her away." Moses did not command divorce but tolerated it under regulation; and the requirement of a written bill, as Augustine noted (under v.1), was itself a restraint — a delay meant to deter the hasty and to give the wise opportunity to reconcile the parties.

The distinction between what Moses "suffered" and what God "commanded" is the hinge of the whole passage. Christ had asked "What did Moses command?" (v.3); the Pharisees answer with what Moses "suffered." The difference exposes their error: they had taken a reluctant permission, granted for hardness of heart, and treated it as a positive sanction for divorce at pleasure.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the Pharisees correctly cited Moses' provision of a bill of divorcement (Deuteronomy 24:1), but their use of it betrayed the lax view (the school of Hillel) that allowed divorce for almost any cause. The careful word "suffered" — permitted, not commanded — both states the truth and exposes their misuse of it: Moses tolerated divorce under regulation, as a restraint on a greater evil, not as a sanction for it. The written bill was itself a curb, requiring deliberation and giving room for reconciliation. Christ would build on this distinction (v.5), showing that the permission was a concession to hardness of heart, not the true mind of God revealed in the creation of marriage (vv.6-9). The verse marks the gap between God's command and Moses' reluctant sufferance.
5:
"And Jesus answered and said unto them, For the hardness of your heart he wrote you this precept." The brief verse — with only Gill in the Catena — gives Christ's decisive interpretation of Moses' permission. Gill notes Christ spoke "with respect to this command, or sufferance of Moses, which they urged."

The key phrase "for the hardness of your heart" the fathers expound at length (under v.1). Augustine: "Moses was against a man's dismissing his wife, for he interposed this delay [the writing of the bill], that a person whose mind was bent on separation, might be deterred by the writing of the bill, and desist." The bill was to be written by the scribes, who "ought to be wise interpreters of the law, and just opponents of quarrel," that they might "by treating between him and his wife persuade them to love and concord." Only if "a hatred so great had arisen that it could not be extinguished" was the bill to be written — "great was the hardness of heart which could not be melted or bent to the taking back and recalling the love of marriage, even by the interposition of a bill." The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary adds: "it is possible for a soul purged from desires and from anger to bear the worst of women; but if those passions have a redoubled force over the mind, many evils will arise from hatred in marriage."

Henry (under v.1) drew the sober application: "none must put away their wives but such as are willing to own that their hearts were so hard as to need this permission." The permission was no honour but a confession of sin; to claim it was to confess one's own hardness of heart.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, the hinge of the divorce teaching. The witnesses agree: Christ traced Moses' permission of divorce to "the hardness of your heart" — a reluctant concession to human sinfulness, designed (as the fathers show) to restrain a greater evil and to give room for reconciliation, not to sanction divorce as good. The written bill was a curb, requiring deliberation and the counsel of the wise; only an unmeltable hardness of heart proceeded to use it. The permission, then, was no positive law but a confession of sin: to claim it was to admit one's own hardness. Christ thus distinguished what Moses tolerated from what God ordained, and prepared to set against the temporary concession the abiding creation ordinance of one flesh (vv.6-9). The lesson is that God's law sometimes regulates an evil it does not approve, condescending to human weakness while pointing beyond it to his true will.
6:
"But from the beginning of the creation God made them male and female." The witnesses note Christ's appeal from Moses' permission back to the creation ordinance. Gill explains the phrase as a common Jewish idiom, "from the beginning of the creation of the world." Barnes cross-references Genesis 1:27; 5:2; Malachi 2:15. Wesley draws an incidental inference: "Therefore Moses in the first of Genesis gives us an account of things from the beginning of the creation... God made Adam, when first created, male only; and Eve female only. And this man and woman he joined together, in a state of innocence, as husband and wife."

Christ's argument the fathers (under v.1) unfold. Chrysostom: "He at once brings back the discourse to the old law, saying, But from the beginning of the creation, God made them male and female. If however he had wished one wife to be put away and another to be brought in, He would have created several women." Bede: God "says not male and females, which the sense would have required had it referred to the divorce of former wives, but male and female, so that they might be bound by the tie of one wife."

The appeal to the beginning is decisive. Against the Pharisees' citation of Moses' permission (a later concession), Christ sets the original institution of marriage at creation — God making one man and one woman, and joining them. The creation ordinance, prior to and deeper than the Mosaic permission, reveals God's true and abiding will for marriage: one man and one woman, for life. That God made "male and female," not several women for one man, shows that monogamy and indissolubility were the design from the first.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ answered the Pharisees not by Moses' permission but by the creation ordinance — "from the beginning God made them male and female." The appeal to the beginning, prior to and deeper than the Mosaic concession, reveals God's true design for marriage: one man joined to one woman. That God made not several women for one man, but one male and one female, condemns both polygamy and divorce at pleasure. Christ thus traces marriage to its divine institution at creation, showing that what God ordained from the first cannot be undone by a later permission granted for hardness of heart. The verse grounds the indissolubility of marriage in the creation itself, and prepares for the conclusion that "what God hath joined together, let not man put asunder" (v.9). The beginning, not the concession, reveals the mind of God.
7:
"For this cause shall a man leave his father and mother, and cleave to his wife." The brief verse cites the foundational text of marriage (Genesis 2:24). Gill notes the citation and that the Persic version's addition ("and brethren and sisters") has no warrant in the text. Barnes cross-references Genesis 2:24. Wesley simply gives the reference.

The verse, quoted by Christ from Genesis 2:24, establishes both the nearness and the permanence of the marriage bond. The fathers (under v.1) drew out its force. Chrysostom: "Nor did God only join one woman to one man, but He also bade a man quit his parents and cleave to his wife... From the very mode of speech, shewing the impossibility of severing marriage, because He said, He shall cleave." The word "cleave" implies an inseparable union — a man so joined to his wife as not to be parted from her.

Henry (under v.1) drew out the implication: "a man must leave his father and mother, and cleave to his wife; which intimates not only the nearness of the relation, but the perpetuity of it; he shall so cleave to his wife as not to be separated from her." That a man leaves even father and mother — the closest natural ties — to cleave to his wife shows how supreme and permanent the marriage bond is. The relation that displaces even parentage is not to be lightly dissolved.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the foundational text of marriage (Genesis 2:24), cited by Christ, establishes both the nearness and the permanence of the bond. That a man leaves even father and mother to cleave to his wife shows the supremacy of the marriage relation over all other natural ties, and the word "cleave" implies an inseparable union, not to be severed. The leaving and the cleaving together mark marriage as a bond that displaces the closest natural relationships and is meant to endure. Christ builds his argument against divorce on this text: if a man so cleaves to his wife as to leave even his parents for her, the union is not to be broken at pleasure. The verse, with v.8, leads to the conclusion that the two become one flesh, and so to the prohibition of v.9. The permanence of marriage is written into its very institution.
8:
"And they twain shall be one flesh: so then they are no more twain, but one flesh." The brief verse — with Gill and Barnes — completes the citation from Genesis 2:24 and draws its conclusion. Gill notes it is "the remaining part of the citation." Barnes cross-references 1 Corinthians 6:16 and Ephesians 5:31, where Paul applies the "one flesh" both to the marriage union and to the mystical union of Christ and the Church.

The phrase "one flesh" the witnesses read as the most intimate possible union. Henry (under v.1): "The result of the relation is, That, though they are two, yet they are one, they are one flesh. The union between them is the most intimate that can be, and... a sacred thing that must not be violated." The two persons, by marriage, become so joined that they are reckoned one — a unity that divorce would tear asunder.

Chrysostom (under v.1) drew out the force of the conclusion: "Being framed out of one root, they will join into one body. It goes on: So then they are no more twain, but one flesh. After this, bringing forward an awful argument, He said not, do not divide, but He concluded, What therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder." The "one flesh" is the ground of the prohibition: to divorce is to divide what God has made one. Bede (under v.1) added the mystical dimension: as the two become one flesh, so the union figures "the mystical inseparable union between Christ and his church."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the conclusion of the creation text — "they are no more twain, but one flesh" — establishes the most intimate and sacred union, which divorce would violate. The two, by marriage, become one; and this unity is the ground of the prohibition that follows (v.9): what God has joined into one flesh, man must not divide. Paul applies the same "one flesh" to the mystical union of Christ and the Church (Ephesians 5:31), so that marriage figures that inseparable bond. The verse completes Christ's argument from creation: marriage is not a contract to be dissolved at will but a God-made unity of two into one, sacred and not to be torn apart. The indissolubility of marriage rests on the "one flesh" that God himself creates in the joining of husband and wife.
9:
"What therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder." The briefest verse of this section — with only Gill's opening words in the Catena — is the climax of Christ's teaching on marriage. The verse draws the conclusion from the creation ordinance (vv.6-8): since God himself joins husband and wife into one flesh, no man may separate them.

The fathers (under v.1) treasure this conclusion. Chrysostom: "bringing forward an awful argument, He said not, do not divide, but He concluded, What therefore God hath joined together, let not man put asunder. But if two persons, whom God has joined together, are not to be separated; much more is it wrong to separate from Christ, the Church, which God has joined to Him." Bede distinguished the agent: "What therefore God hath conjoined by making one flesh of a man and a woman, that man cannot separate, but God alone."

Henry (under v.1) drew out the inference: "from all this he infers, that men ought not to put their wives asunder from them, whom God has put so near them. The bond which God himself has tied, is not to be lightly untied." That it is God who joins husband and wife — not merely as Creator fitting them for each other, but as the Author of marriage appointing them to live together till death — makes divorce a presumptuous undoing of God's own work.

The thin comment matches the shortest verse, the climax of the divorce teaching. The witnesses agree: the conclusion crowns the whole argument — since God himself joins husband and wife into one flesh, man must not put them asunder. The marriage bond is not a human arrangement to be dissolved at will but a union tied by God, and to divorce is to presume to undo God's own work. Christ does not merely counsel against division but pronounces against it as a violation of the divine institution. The fathers extend the principle: if those whom God has joined are not to be separated, much more is it wrong to separate the Church from Christ, whom God has joined to him. The verse stands as the abiding charter of marriage's indissolubility, grounded not in human sentiment but in the joining act of God. What God has tied, let no man untie.
10:
"And in the house his disciples asked him again of the same matter." The brief verse — with Gill and Ellicott — records the disciples' private inquiry after the public confrontation. Gill notes the house "to which Christ retired, after he had put the Pharisees to silence, and dismissed the multitude." Ellicott marks the detail as peculiar to Mark: "St. Mark's narrative is, on the whole, much shorter than St. Matthew's; but this detail of the question coming from the disciples after they had entered the house is given by him only."

The disciples' renewed questioning the fathers (under v.1) read as a sign of their not being fully satisfied. Theophylact: "the disciples were offended, as not being fully satisfied with what had been said; for this reason they again question Him." Pseudo-Jerome: "This second question is said to be asked again by the Apostles, because it is on the subject of which the Pharisees had asked Him." The Gloss adds that the repetition of the Word "produces not weariness, but thirst and hunger... for the tasting of the honied words of wisdom yields all manner of savour to them who love her."

The private setting — "in the house" — the witnesses note as the place of fuller instruction. Henry (under v.1): "It was an advantage to them, that they had opportunity of personal converse with Christ, not only about gospel mysteries, but about moral duties, for further satisfaction." Apart from the crowd and the hostile Pharisees, the disciples could press Christ for a clearer understanding of his teaching on marriage.

The thin comment matches the brief verse, which marks the transition from public to private teaching. The witnesses agree: the disciples, not fully satisfied by the public exchange, asked Christ again privately in the house about marriage and divorce — a detail given only by Mark, drawn (it is likely) from Peter's memory. The private setting was the place of fuller instruction, where the disciples could press their Master for clearer understanding apart from the crowd. Their renewed questioning, far from wearying Christ, gave occasion for the plain statement of vv.11-12, that divorce and remarriage is adultery. The verse illustrates the value of personal converse with Christ for the deeper grasp of his teaching, and the readiness of the disciples to seek further light on a hard saying. The honied words of wisdom, repeated, breed not weariness but hunger in those who love her.
11:
"And he saith unto them, Whosoever shall put away his wife, and marry another, committeth adultery against her." The witnesses note Christ's plain statement to the disciples and Mark's omission of the exception. Gill cross-references Matthew 5:32; 19:9. Robertson: "Mark does not give the exception stated in Matthew 19:9 except for fornication which see for discussion, though the point is really involved in what Mark does record. Mere formal divorce does not annul actual marriage consummated by the physical union. Breaking that bond does annul it." Wesley: "All polygamy is here totally condemned."

The statement "committeth adultery against her" the witnesses read as defining the nature of the wrong. Henry (under v.1): "it is adultery for a man to put away his wife, and marry another; it is adultery against the wife he puts away, it is a wrong to her, a breach of his contract with her." The man who divorces his wife to marry another wrongs the first wife, breaking the bond of one flesh that God had tied; and his new union, the first marriage still standing in God's sight, is adultery.

The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary (under v.1) had explained: "The Lord calls by the name of adultery cohabitation with her who is not a man's wife; she is not, however, a wife, whom a man has taken to him, after quitting the first; and for this reason he commits adultery upon her, that is, upon the second, whom he brings in." The second union is no true marriage, the first bond being still in force.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ stated plainly to his disciples that a man who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery against her — wronging the first wife by breaking the one-flesh bond God had tied, and entering a union that, the first marriage still standing, is adultery. Mark omits the Matthean exception ("except for fornication"), though the principle is implied: only the breaking of the marriage bond by unfaithfulness annuls it, mere formal divorce does not. The statement defines the wrong as adultery "against her" — a breach of covenant and an injury to the wife. Christ thus upholds the indissolubility taught from creation (vv.6-9): divorce at pleasure does not free a man to remarry but makes his remarriage adulterous. The verse, with v.12, applies the creation ordinance to the practical question, condemning the lax divorce of the day.
12:
"And if a woman shall put away her husband, and be married to another, she committeth adultery." The witnesses note this clause as peculiar to Mark, addressing the wife's divorce of her husband. Gill notes there was no such permission in the law of Moses for a woman to divorce her husband; if practised, it was borrowed from the Gentiles "about this time, in their declining state." Ellicott: "This also is peculiar to St. Mark, and it is noticeable, as being the only passage in our Lord's teaching which distinctly states the case referred to, and passes sentence on the wife who divorces her husband." Robertson notes the Roman context: "Greek and Roman law allowed the divorce of the husband by the wife though not provided for in Jewish law. But the thing was sometimes done as in the case of Herodias... So also Salome, Herod's sister, divorced her husband. Both Bruce and Gould think that Mark added this item to the words of Jesus for the benefit of the Gentile environment of this Roman Gospel."

The mutual application — condemning both the husband who divorces and the wife who divorces — the witnesses note as a mark of the equality of the marriage bond. Ellicott: "All three cases are dealt with on the same grounds: (1) that the marriage relationship ought to be indissoluble, and that one cause only justifies or permits its dissolution; and (2) that any further permission of divorce is but a concession to the hardness of men's hearts." Clarke notes the principle: "where it is equally balanced, the right being the same on each side, it must serve as a mutual check."

Henry (under v.1) drew out that the wife's consent is no excuse: "If a woman shall put away her husband, that is, elope from him, leave him by consent, and be married to another, she commits adultery, and it will be no excuse at all for her to say that it was with the consent of her husband."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Mark alone records Christ's condemnation of the wife who divorces her husband and remarries — applying the law of marriage equally to both sexes. The clause, fitting Mark's Roman readers (where, unlike Jewish law, a wife could divorce her husband, as Herodias and Salome had done), shows that the marriage bond binds husband and wife alike, and that neither may break it at pleasure. The mutual application establishes the equality of the bond: what is adultery in the husband is adultery in the wife. Even the consent of the other party is no excuse, for the bond is tied by God, not by mutual agreement (v.9). The verse extends Christ's teaching to the Gentile world, where the wife's divorce was common, and upholds the indissolubility of marriage for both.
13:
"And they brought young children to him, that he should touch them: and his disciples rebuked those that brought them." The witnesses note the faith of those who brought the children and the disciples' mistaken rebuke. The Antiochene and Latin fathers read it richly. Theophylact: "the great faith of the multitude, who believed that Christ conferred a blessing on the children whom they brought to Him, by the mere laying on of His hands." Chrysostom: "the disciples, out of regard for the dignity of Christ, forbade those who brought them. But our Saviour, in order to teach His disciples to be modest in their ideas, and to tread under foot worldly pride, takes the children to Him." Origen reads it for ministers: "If any of those who profess to hold the office of teaching in the Church should see a person bringing to them some of the foolish of this world... let him not forbid the man who offers such an one to the Saviour."

On "of such is the kingdom," the fathers expound childlike simplicity. Theophylact: "for a child does not hate, does nothing of evil intent, nor though beaten does he quit his mother." Bede: "if ye have not innocence and purity of mind like that of children, ye cannot enter into the kingdom of heaven. Or else, we are ordered to receive the kingdom of God... as a little child, because as a child, when he is taught, does not contradict his teachers... but receives with faith what they teach." Origen: "the mind of a child is pure from all passions."

The puritan Henry (covering vv.13-16): the disciples rebuked them, "thinking that they did not become disciples of Christ to spend their time, and crowd up his lodgings, with such useless things as little children." But Christ "was much displeased... he took it ill of them that they should keep any from him that came, or were brought."

The witnesses agree: the children were brought in faith that Christ's touch would bless them, but the disciples, supposing this beneath their Master's dignity, rebuked those who brought them — an error Christ would at once correct (v.14). The fathers read the children as a figure of the humble, simple, and teachable, of whom is the kingdom; and the disciples' rebuke as a worldly pride that Christ would tread underfoot. The bringing of children to Christ for blessing is commended as an act of faith; the hindering of them, a fault. The verse sets up Christ's tender welcome of the children and his great lesson that the kingdom is received only by those who become as little children (v.15).
14:
"But when Jesus saw it, he was much displeased, and said unto them, Suffer the little children to come unto me, and forbid them not: for of such is the kingdom of God." The witnesses note the strength of Christ's displeasure and the welcome he gives the children. Robertson stresses the word: "He was moved with indignation. In Mark alone. The word is ingressive aorist, became indignant, and is a strong word of deep emotion." Ellicott notes the word "as used by our Lord, is peculiar to St. Mark." Barnes gives the reasons: "first, it was a pleasure to him to receive and bless little children; and, secondly, they [the disciples] were doing what they were not commanded to do." Robertson draws the application: "Surely it ought to be a joy to parents to bring their children to Jesus... but to hinder their coming is a crime. There are parents who will have to give answer to God for keeping their children away from Jesus."

The strength of Christ's indignation the witnesses note as a measure of his love for children. Wesley: "Jesus seeing it was much displeased — at their blaming those who were not blame worthy: and endeavouring to hinder the children from receiving a blessing." Christ's strong emotion was directed at the disciples' interference with those he delighted to bless.

On "of such is the kingdom," the witnesses (following the fathers, under v.13) read it of the childlike. Wesley: "The members of the kingdom which I am come to set up in the world are such as these, as well as grown persons, of a child-like temper." The kingdom belongs to children and to those who share their humble, simple, teachable spirit.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ, seeing the disciples rebuke those who brought the children, was strongly indignant — Mark alone records the depth of his emotion — and welcomed the children with "Suffer the little children to come unto me, and forbid them not." His displeasure measured his love for the children and his abhorrence of those who would hinder any from coming to him. The saying "of such is the kingdom of God" claims the kingdom for children and for all of childlike temper — humble, simple, and teachable. The verse rebukes the disciples' worldly pride and tender welcomes the little ones, teaching that parents should bring their children to Christ and that none should keep them away. To hinder a child from Christ is, Robertson warns, a crime for which parents must answer. Christ's arms are open to the least.
15:
"Verily I say unto you, Whosoever shall not receive the kingdom of God as a little child, he shall not enter therein." The witnesses note the solemn asseveration and the condition of entering the kingdom. Gill notes the form "verily I say unto you" is used "when our Lord was about to asseverate a thing, and assert something of moment." Ellicott: "To receive the kingdom of God as a little child, is to receive it after the manner of a child, with simplicity and faith, humility and love." Barnes: "With the temper and spirit of a child — teachable, mild, humble, and free from prejudice and obstinacy."

On how a child receives the kingdom, Robertson explains: "The little child learns to obey its parents simply and uncomplainingly... Jesus here presents the little child with trusting and simple and loving obedience as the model for adults in coming into the kingdom. Jesus does not here say that children are in the kingdom of God because they are children." Wesley: "As totally disclaiming all worthiness and fitness, as if he were but a week old." The childlike reception is one of humble dependence, simple faith, and trusting obedience — disclaiming all worthiness in oneself.

The condition is absolute: "he shall not enter therein." Barnes: "Shall not be a Christian; shall not be a real member of the family of Christ on earth, though he may be a professor; and shall never enter heaven." The kingdom cannot be received by the proud, the self-sufficient, or the obstinate, but only by those who become as little children. This was a rebuke to the disciples, who (in the parallels) were disputing who should be greatest; as Ellicott notes, "those who were disputing who was the greatest in it, were as if they had not even entered it."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ solemnly declared that the kingdom of God can be received only as a little child receives it — with simplicity, humility, faith, and trusting obedience, disclaiming all self-worthiness. The condition is absolute: whoever will not so receive it shall not enter. The childlike reception is not innocence merely but humble dependence and teachable faith, the opposite of the pride and self-sufficiency that bar the kingdom. The saying both welcomes the lowly and rebukes the proud — including the disciples who disputed about greatness. To enter the kingdom, one must become as a child: small, dependent, trusting, obedient. The verse makes childlikeness not an option but the very condition of entrance, and so exalts the humble while excluding the proud. (The Cyril fragment here, on eternal life by faith, touches the theme but is a broader catechetical aside.)
16:
"And he took them up in his arms, put his hands upon them, and blessed them." The witnesses note the tenderness of Christ's gesture and the reality of the blessing. Gill: "it expresses great tenderness towards them, and affection for them." Robertson: "A distinct rebuke to the protest of the over-particular disciples... with tender fondling Jesus repeatedly blessed (imperfect), laying his hands upon each of them. It was a great moment for each mother and child." Ellicott: "Better, folded them in His arms... The actual blessing, though implied in St. Matthew, is also definitely mentioned by him [Mark] only."

The grammatical-historical Barnes draws out the lesson for parents: "How happy would it be if all parents thus felt it to be their privilege to present their children to Christ! The question with a parent should be, not whether he ought to present them by prayer, but whether he may do it." He applies it (with Clarke) to the privilege of devoting children to God — Clarke arguing pointedly for infant baptism: "If Christ embraced them, why should not his Church embrace them? Why not dedicate them to God by baptism?"

The fathers (under v.13) read the embrace richly. The Pseudo-Chrysostom commentary: "Fitly does He take them up into His arms to bless them, as it were, lifting into His own bosom, and reconciling Himself to His creation, which in the beginning fell from Him." Bede: "Having embraced the children, He also blessed them, implying that the lowly in spirit are worthy of His blessing, grace and love."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ took the children into his arms, laid his hands on each, and blessed them — a gesture of great tenderness and a real conferring of blessing (mentioned by Mark alone). The embrace, repeated for each child, was both a rebuke to the disciples who would have kept them away and a model for parents, who are privileged to bring their children to Christ. The fathers read the embrace as Christ reconciling to himself the creation that had fallen, and as a sign that the lowly in spirit are worthy of his grace. Barnes and Clarke draw the application to the dedication of children to God, Clarke pressing infant baptism. The verse closes the scene with Christ's tender welcome of the little ones, displaying both his love for children and the truth that his blessing rests on the humble. The arms that embraced the children are open to all who come as children.
17:
"And when he was gone forth into the way, there came one running, and kneeled to him, and asked him, Good Master, what shall I do that I may inherit eternal life?" The witnesses note the eager, reverent approach of the rich young ruler and the seriousness of his question. Robertson: "Jesus had left the house and was proceeding with the caravan on the way when this ruler eagerly ran and kneeled and was asking Jesus about his problem. Both these details alone in Mark." Lightfoot details the kneeling as the posture of one taking hold of and kissing the knees in reverence.

The puritan Henry (covering vv.17-31) marks the promising features: "He came running to Christ, which was an indication of his humility... he ran as one in haste, and longing to be in conversation with Christ." He came openly, "in the midst of company," not by night like Nicodemus; "he kneeled to him, in token of the great value and veneration he had for him." His question was "serious and weighty... Eternal life was an article of his creed... he asks for good to be done in this world, in order to the enjoyment of the greatest good in the other world." Henry commends it: "Then there begins to be some hope of people, when they begin to enquire solicitously, what they shall do to get to heaven."

Bede (under v.17) takes the man's question and confession as sincere, not tempting: "We must not suppose that this man either asked the Lord, with a wish to tempt Him... or lied in his account of his life; but we must believe that he confessed with simplicity how he had lived." Theophylact, however, marks the flaw beneath: "I wonder at this young man, who when all others come to Christ to be healed... begs of Him the possession of everlasting life, notwithstanding his love of money, the malignant passion which afterwards caused his sorrow."

The witnesses agree: the rich young ruler came to Christ with every outward mark of earnestness, humility, and reverence — running, kneeling, asking the most serious of questions about eternal life. His approach was promising, and Christ would deal kindly with him (v.21). Yet beneath the fair appearance lay the love of money that would prove his undoing (v.22). The verse opens one of the most poignant encounters in the Gospels: a young man with much that was admirable, sincerely seeking eternal life, yet held back by the one thing he would not surrender. His eager question — "what shall I do that I may inherit eternal life?" — sets the stage for Christ's testing answer.
18:
"And Jesus said unto him, Why callest thou me good? there is none good but one, that is, God." The witnesses note Christ's probing reply to the title "Good Master." Gill cross-references Matthew 19:17. Ellicott: "Our Lord's question is, in St. Mark's report, in harmony with that of the seeker after life eternal. Its obvious drift was to force him back upon the conditions of absolute goodness, to make him ask himself how far... that word might be used relatively of any child of man." Robertson: "Jesus challenges him to define his attitude towards him as was proper. Did he mean good in the absolute sense as applied to God? The language is not a disclaiming of deity on the part of Jesus."

The fathers (under v.17) divide on the emphasis, but agree Christ does not deny his own goodness or deity. Bede: "The Lord, therefore, does not deny Himself to be good, but implies that He is God; He does not deny that He is good Master, but He declares that no master is good but God." Chrysostom, noting the man came to Christ "as he would to a man, and to one of the Jewish doctors," explains: "Christ answered him as Man... In saying which He does not exclude men from goodness, but from a comparison with the goodness of God."

Henry (under v.17) read it as assisting the man's faith: Christ "would have him mean thereby, that he looked upon him to be God, since there is none good but one, that is God." The question was meant to make the young man consider whether, in calling Christ "good," he was confessing his deity — for absolute goodness belongs to God alone.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ's question "Why callest thou me good?" was not a denial of his own goodness or deity but a probe — forcing the young man to consider what he meant by the title. If "good" be taken in the absolute sense, it belongs to God alone; and so, the fathers note, Christ implies that he is God, or challenges the man to recognize it. Bede and Henry read it as leading the ruler toward confessing Christ's deity; Chrysostom, that Christ answered as man one who approached him as a mere human teacher, excluding men not from goodness but from comparison with God's goodness. Either way, the question is no disclaimer of deity but an invitation to deeper understanding. Christ turns the man's casual compliment into a test of his apprehension, pointing him to the absolute goodness of God and, beneath the surface, to his own divine nature. The verse opens Christ's dealing with the seeker by directing him to God as the standard of good.
19:
"Thou knowest the commandments, Do not commit adultery, Do not kill, Do not steal, Do not bear false witness, Defraud not, Honour thy father and mother." The witnesses note Christ's pointing the young man to the commandments and the distinctive "Defraud not." Gill notes the commandments "God gave to Moses on Mount Sinai; these the Jews taught their children." Barnes explains "Defraud not": "Do not take away your neighbour's property by fraud or dishonesty... It is thus a violation of the ninth and tenth commandments; and our Saviour very properly, therefore, condensed the two." Ellicott reads it as specially aimed at the rich man: "It seems as if intended to be a special application of the Tenth Commandment. One who had great possessions... needed to examine himself specially by that text. Were there no ill-gotten gains in his treasure? Had no wages of the reaper been kept back; no sharp bargains driven with widows or orphans or the poor?"

Christ's pointing to the commandments the witnesses read (with Bede, under v.17) as setting before the man the righteousness of the law: "Thou knowest the commandments, Do not commit adultery, Do not kill; for this is the childlike blamelessness which is proposed to us, if we would enter the kingdom of heaven." The commandments named are those of the second table, concerning duty to one's neighbour — the visible, testable righteousness.

Henry (under v.17) noted the order and the substitution: Christ "mentions the six commandments of the second table... he inverts the order, putting the seventh commandment before the sixth, to intimate that adultery is a sin no less heinous than murder. The fifth commandment is here put last... And instead of the tenth commandment, Thou shalt not covet, our Saviour here puts, Defraud not" — a fitting probe for a rich man.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ directed the young man to the commandments of the second table — the testable righteousness toward one's neighbour — and pointedly included "Defraud not," a special application of the tenth commandment aimed at one whose wealth might have been gathered by sharp dealing. By naming these commands, Christ set before the man the standard of the law, that he might examine himself; and the substitution of "Defraud not" for "Thou shalt not covet" laid the finger on the very temptation of the rich. The commandments named are those whose keeping is outwardly verifiable, so that the man's claim (v.20) could be weighed. Christ's answer meets the man on his own ground — "what shall I do?" — by pointing first to the law, before exposing the one thing he lacked (v.21). The verse probes the rich man precisely where his wealth made him vulnerable.
20:
"And he answered and said unto him, Master, all these have I observed from my youth." The brief verse records the young man's confident claim to have kept the commandments. Gill notes he answered "with a great deal of pertness" (that is, promptness or assurance). Robertson: "Literally, these all (of them)." Wesley marks a telling detail: "He stands reproved now, and drops the epithet good" — calling Christ simply "Master," no longer "Good Master," perhaps stung by Christ's question (v.18).

The young man's claim the witnesses receive with a measure of charity and a measure of caution. Bede (under v.17) took it as sincere: "We must not suppose that this man... lied in his account of his life; but we must believe that he confessed with simplicity how he had lived." Yet the claim revealed a shallow understanding of the law. Henry (under v.17): "He thought he had, and his neighbours thought so too. Note, Ignorance of the extent and spiritual nature of the divine law, makes people think themselves in a better condition than they really are. Paul was alive without the law. But when he saw that to be spiritual, he saw himself to be carnal" (Romans 7:9, 14).

The claim "from my youth" the witnesses note as both an evidence of an outwardly moral life and a sign of self-ignorance. Henry: "he that could say he was free from scandalous sin, went further than many in the way to eternal life. But though we know nothing by ourselves, yet are we not thereby justified" (1 Corinthians 4:4). The man's outward blamelessness was real as far as it went, but his confidence in it betrayed that he had not grasped the law's spiritual depth.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the young man confidently claimed to have kept all the commandments from his youth — a claim sincere in intent and evidencing an outwardly moral life, yet betraying a shallow grasp of the law's spiritual extent. Had he understood the law as reaching the heart (as Paul came to see, Romans 7), he would not have thought himself blameless. His outward righteousness, real as far as it went, made him think himself nearer the kingdom than he was; ignorance of the law's depth bred self-confidence. Yet his very seriousness — asking what he lacked still — left room for hope. Christ would now lay the finger on the one thing his claim concealed: his bondage to riches (v.21). The verse displays the self-ignorance of the merely moral man, who, knowing no scandalous sin, supposes himself whole, not seeing the idol in his heart.
21:
"Then Jesus beholding him loved him, and said unto him, One thing thou lackest: go thy way, sell whatsoever thou hast, and give to the poor, and thou shalt have treasure in heaven: and come, take up the cross, and follow me." The witnesses treasure Mark's unique note that Christ "loved him," and probe the testing command. Robertson: "Mark alone mentions this glance of affection... Jesus fell in love with this charming youth." Ellicott: "the love showed itself in the stedfast look, perhaps also in the kiss upon the brow with which the Rabbis of the time showed their approval." Lightfoot documents the rabbinic custom of kissing the head of a promising disciple. Clarke: Christ loved him "because of his youth, his earnestness, and his sincerity."

The Reformed Calvin gives the fullest treatment of how Christ could "love" a man who would not follow. He distinguishes degrees of God's love: "God embraces in fatherly love none but his children... and it is in consequence of this love that they are accepted at his tribunal," yet "God is sometimes said to love those whom he does not approve or justify; for, since the preservation of the human race is agreeable to Him... he is said to love the political virtues; not that they are meritorious... but that they have reference to an end of which he approves." On the command, Calvin: Christ "does not mean that the [perfection lies] beyond the keeping of the law, but in the very keeping of the law," for "the rich man is very far from keeping it fully, so long as he is attached to his riches, and burns with covetousness." He warns against the monks who claim "perfection" from this text: "Christ does not command all without exception to sell all."

The fathers (under v.17) read the command as exposing the man's idol. Theophylact: "there is a great difference between having riches, and loving them." Cyril of Jerusalem, on-topic, defends wealth rightly used: "Riches, and gold, and silver are not, as some think, the devil's... Do thou but use it well, and there is no fault to be found with money... a man may even be justified by money: I was hungry, and ye gave Me meat... And wouldest thou learn that money may become a door of the kingdom of heaven? Sell that thou hast, and give to the poor."

The witnesses agree: Christ, looking on the young man with genuine love (a note peculiar to Mark), laid his finger on the one thing he lacked — bidding him sell all, give to the poor, take up the cross, and follow. The command was a touchstone, to test whether he loved God above his wealth. The "love" of Christ is read by Calvin as a real but lesser love for the man's moral virtues, distinct from saving love. The command exposed the idol of riches; and the man's response (v.22) would reveal that, for all his earnestness, he loved his possessions more than eternal life. The cross laid upon him was the surrender of the one thing he would not give.
22:
"And he was sad at that saying, and went away grieved: for he had great possessions." The witnesses note the young man's sorrowful refusal and its cause. Gill: "he was sad at that saying, that he lacked one thing, and especially that he should be bid to sell all that he had, and give it away; and what might add to his sadness is, that he must take up the cross." Robertson parses the vivid word: "But his countenance fell. The verb is from sombre, gloomy, like a lowering cloud." Ellicott: "Better, He frowned." Clarke laments: "This young man had perhaps been a saint, and an eminent apostle, had he been poor! From this... we may learn that it is oftentimes a misfortune to be rich."

The cause — "for he had great possessions" — the witnesses note as the root of his failure. Henry (under v.17): "That which ruined this young man was, he had great possessions; thus the prosperity of fools destroys them." The very abundance that the world counts a blessing was his snare; his wealth held his heart, and he could not part with it even for eternal life.

The man's grief the witnesses read as the proof of his bondage. Henry: "He was sad at that saying; was sorry that he could not be a follower of Christ upon any easier terms than leaving all to follow him; that he could not lay hold on eternal life, and keep hold of his temporal possessions too." He went away grieved — not angry, but sorrowful, for he genuinely desired eternal life yet could not bring himself to surrender his idol. "While he held to mammon he did in effect despise Christ" (Henry; cf. Matthew 6:24).

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the young man, faced with the command to sell all, went away grieved, for he had great possessions — and his grief revealed that, for all his earnestness, he loved his wealth more than eternal life. His sorrow was the proof of his bondage: he desired the prize but could not pay the price, could not part with his idol. The very riches the world counts a blessing were his ruin, holding his heart so that he could not follow Christ. He did not refuse in anger but in sorrow, knowing what he forfeited; yet he forfeited it still. The verse is one of the saddest in the Gospels: a man with much that was admirable, sincerely seeking life, turning away because he would not surrender the one thing that held him. "Ye cannot serve God and mammon" (Matthew 6:24); the choice, once forced, exposed where his heart truly lay.
23:
"And Jesus looked round about, and saith unto his disciples, How hardly shall they that have riches enter into the kingdom of God!" The witnesses note Christ's searching look and the hard saying drawn from the young man's departure. Robertson: "Another picture of the looks of Jesus and in Mark alone... When the man was gone the Lord's eye swept round the circle of the Twelve, as he drew for them the lesson of the incident." Gill: he looked round "to see what effect the discourse he had with the young man... had upon his disciples; as also to raise their attention." Ellicott notes "the glance and gesture are mentioned by St. Mark only."

The saying "How hardly shall they that have riches enter" the witnesses read as the lesson of the rich young ruler's failure. Henry (covering vv.23-31): "Christ took this occasion to speak to his disciples about the difficulty of the salvation of those who have abundance of this world... How hardly shall they who have riches enter into the kingdom of God! They have many temptations to grapple with, and many difficulties to get over, which lie not in the way of poor people." The difficulty lies not in the riches themselves but in the hold they take on the heart, as Christ would clarify (v.24): the danger is in trusting in riches.

Henry draws the application: "Those who thus eagerly seek the wealth of the world, will never rightly prize Christ and his grace... Let us learn contentment in a low state, and to watch against the love of riches in a high one."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ, looking round on his disciples after the young man's departure, drew the lesson — "How hardly shall they that have riches enter into the kingdom of God!" The searching look (peculiar to Mark) gathered the disciples' attention to the sober truth illustrated by the rich ruler's refusal. The difficulty lies not in riches as such but in the hold they take on the heart, breeding a trust and love that crowds out Christ (clarified in v.24). The rich have many temptations and difficulties that lie not in the poor man's way; their abundance becomes a snare, as it had for the young ruler. The saying both explains the man's failure and warns the disciples (and us) against the love of wealth, whether possessed or merely desired. Christ turns a single sad encounter into a lasting lesson on the spiritual peril of riches. (The Cyril fragment here, on baptism, is an off-topic catechetical aside.)
24:
"And the disciples were astonished at his words. But Jesus answereth again, and saith unto them, Children, how hard is it for them that trust in riches to enter into the kingdom of God!" The witnesses note the disciples' astonishment and Christ's tender clarification. Gill explains their surprise: "they expected, in a little while, that the kingdom of the Messiah would be set up in great worldly pomp and grandeur; and that all the rich men of the nation would come into it." Robertson: "A look of blank astonishment was on their faces... They in common with other Jews regarded wealth as a token of God's special favour." Barnes notes the address "Children" is "an expression of affection, perhaps also implying a reproof that their slowness of understanding was like children."

On the textual question of "them that trust in riches," the witnesses differ. Ellicott and Robertson note the phrase is absent from the best manuscripts. Ellicott: "there is good reason for thinking, as they are wanting in the best MSS., that they were added by some one who sought to tone down the words of warning... Omitting the doubtful words, the sentence runs, How hard is it to enter into the kingdom of God! — hard alike for rich and poor." Robertson: "Westcott and Hort omit them from their text as an evident addition to explain the difficult words of Jesus." Yet the phrase, whether original or an early gloss, captures Christ's meaning, as Henry (under v.17) and Wesley affirm: "the danger arose not so much from their having riches as from their trusting to them" (Henry); "it is easier for a camel to go through a needle's eye, than for a man to have riches, and not trust in them" (Wesley).

The tender address "Children" the witnesses note as softening the hard truth without retracting it. Wesley: "See how he softens the harsh truth, by the manner of delivering it! And yet without retracting or abating one tittle."

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the disciples, who like other Jews counted wealth a mark of God's favour, were astonished at Christ's saying; and Christ, addressing them tenderly as "Children," clarified (or, in the disputed phrase, was clarified) that the peril lies in trusting in riches. Whether the words "that trust in riches" are original or an early gloss, they capture his meaning: the danger is not the having but the trusting, the heart's reliance on wealth for security and happiness. The hard saying is hard alike for rich and poor, for the love of riches can grip those who have little as well as those who have much. Christ softens the truth by his tender manner without abating it. The verse both reveals the disciples' worldly expectations and corrects them, locating the spiritual danger of wealth precisely in the trust it tempts the heart to give. (The Cyril fragment here, on baptism, is an off-topic catechetical aside.)
25:
"It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle, than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God." The witnesses note the vivid proverb and its force. Gill cross-references the parallel. Robertson discusses the words: "Both Matthew and Mark employ the word for needle from sew... Mark's word [for eye] is from to wear away, to perforate." Luke, the physician, "uses the surgical needle." The image is of the largest beast passing through the smallest opening — a deliberate hyperbole for impossibility by human means.

The witnesses report (chiefly through Henry, under v.17) two attempts to soften the image, while leaning toward the plain hyperbole. Henry: "Some imagine there might be some wicket-gate, or door, to Jerusalem, commonly known by the name of the needle's eye... through which a camel could not be got, unless he were unloaded, and made to kneel." Others "suggest that the word we translate a camel, sometimes signifies a cable-rope." But Henry notes these attempts to "bring the camel and the eye of the needle a little nearer together" miss the point, for "the more it is so the more it answers the intention" — the very impossibility is the lesson. Theophylact (under v.17) had allowed both readings ("the animal itself, or else that thick cable") but the force is the same.

The proverb's meaning the witnesses fix as the human impossibility of a rich man's salvation — which v.27 will resolve by the power of God. The rich man, trusting in his wealth (v.24), cannot by his own strength enter the kingdom, any more than a camel can pass through a needle's eye.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Christ's vivid proverb — the camel and the needle's eye — proclaims the human impossibility of a rich man's entering the kingdom while he trusts in his riches. The image of the largest beast and the smallest opening is a deliberate hyperbole for what cannot be done by human means. The attempts to soften it (a "needle's eye" gate, or a "cable" for "camel") miss the point, for the very impossibility is the lesson, magnified the more by the disproportion. The rich man, his heart held by his wealth, cannot save himself; his case is humanly hopeless. Yet the proverb is not the last word: Christ will at once add that what is impossible with men is possible with God (v.27). The verse drives the disciples (and us) to despair of human means and to look to the grace of God, which alone can bring a rich man — or any sinner — through the needle's eye into the kingdom. (The Cyril fragment here, on baptism, is an off-topic catechetical aside.)
26:
"And they were astonished out of measure, saying among themselves, Who then can be saved?" The brief verse records the disciples' deepened astonishment and despairing question. Gill: "They were still more amazed, their surprise increased exceedingly upon our Lord's using the above comparison; which, in their apprehension, showed, that it was utterly impossible for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God." Barnes: "Out of measure. Very much, or exceedingly."

The disciples' question "Who then can be saved?" the witnesses read as the natural inference from Christ's saying, and the measure of their bewilderment. Henry (under v.17): "They were astonished out of measure, and said among themselves, Who then can be saved? They knew what were generally the sentiments of the Jewish teachers, who affirmed that the Spirit of God chooses to reside in rich men; nay, they knew what abundance of promises there were, in the Old Testament, of temporal good things... and therefore were amazed to hear that it should be so hard for rich people to go to heaven."

The despairing question, the witnesses note, reasoned (rightly) that if the rich — who seemed most favoured and best able to do good — could hardly be saved, then salvation must be hard for all. Their question reaches beyond the rich to the whole human case: if wealth, which the world counts a blessing and the means of much good, is so great a snare, who then can be saved? The question prepares for Christ's answer (v.27), that salvation is impossible with men but possible with God.

The thin comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the disciples, astonished beyond measure at the camel-and-needle saying, despaired — "Who then can be saved?" Their question, born of the Jewish view that wealth was a token of God's favour and the means of doing good, reasoned that if even the rich could hardly enter the kingdom, salvation must be hard indeed for all. The despairing inquiry reaches beyond the rich to the whole human race: if the most favoured can scarcely be saved, what hope for any? The question is the right one, for it drives the disciples from all confidence in human advantage — wealth, status, or works — to the grace of God. Christ's answer (v.27) meets the despair: with men it is impossible, but not with God, for with God all things are possible. The verse marks the depth of the disciples' bewilderment, which only the power of God can resolve, and prepares for the great word that salvation is God's work, not man's.
27:
"And Jesus looking upon them saith, With men it is impossible, but not with God: for with God all things are possible." The witnesses read this as the answer to the disciples' astonishment that the rich can scarcely be saved. The grammatical-historical Gill notes that Christ, "looking upon them, by their countenances saw the surprise and anxiety of mind they were in, as well as by his omniscience knew their private reasonings." Robertson observes "their amazement increased," and presses the contrast: "the impossible by the side of men becomes possible by the side of God" — adding pointedly that this "brushes to one side all petty theories of a gate called needle's eye, etc."

Barnes cross-references the great affirmations of God's omnipotence — "is anything too hard for the Lord?" (Genesis 18:14), "I know that thou canst do every thing" (Job 42:2), "there is nothing too hard for thee" (Jeremiah 32:17), "with God nothing shall be impossible" (Luke 1:37). The saving of a rich man, humanly hopeless, is no obstacle to the grace that can change the heart and loosen the hold of riches.

Cyril of Jerusalem turns the word to the hope of eternal life: "we must not disbelieve the possibility of this, but having an eye not to our own weakness but to His power, we must believe; for with God all things are possible." So the witnesses make the verse the great consolation set against the hard saying about wealth. The disciples, hearing that the rich can hardly enter the kingdom, ask in dismay, "Who then can be saved?" — and Christ answers by lifting their eyes from human impossibility to divine power. The lesson the expositors press is that salvation, of the rich or of any sinner, is never the achievement of man but the work of God, with whom "all things are possible." Robertson's insistence that no literal "needle's-eye gate" is meant guards the saying's force: it is not that wealth makes salvation merely difficult, to be eased by some narrow passage, but that it makes salvation, by human reckoning, impossible — and possible only to the omnipotent grace that can do what no man can. The verse thus turns the despair of the disciples into the ground of hope, that the God for whom nothing is too hard can save even those whom riches most entangle.
28:
"Then Peter began to say unto him, Lo, we have left all, and have followed thee." The witnesses read Peter's words as a question about reward, prompted by the rich young man's refusal. The Latin Gloss explains the occasion: "because the youth, on hearing the advice concerning casting away his goods, had gone away sorrowful, the disciples, who had already fulfilled the precept, began to question him concerning their reward, thinking they had done a great thing." Theophylact notes the modesty of what Peter calls "all": "although Peter had left but few things, still he calls these his all; for even a few things keep us by the bond of affection, so that he shall be beatified who leaves a few things."

Bede draws out the two parts of Peter's claim: "because it is not sufficient to have left all, he adds that which makes up perfection, and have followed thee — as if he said, We have done what Thou hast commanded; what reward therefore wilt Thou give us?" The leaving of all is completed by the following of Christ; the one without the other is not enough.

So the witnesses make the verse the disciples' appeal for the promise that the rich young man forfeited. Where he went away sorrowful, unwilling to part with his goods, the Twelve had left their little all and followed; and Peter, speaking for them, asks what they shall have. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, with Theophylact, that the worth of the sacrifice lies not in its amount but in the affection it costs: the disciples' few things were their "all," and the leaving of even a little, when it is all one has and holds one by the bond of love, is counted by Christ a great thing. Second, with Bede, that true discipleship is not merely renouncing but following — leaving all for Christ and cleaving to Christ. Peter's question, though it betrays a trace of self-regard (as the closing "first shall be last" will gently check), is not rebuked by Christ but answered with a great promise; for the Lord who saw the rich man turn away will not let the faithful poor go unrewarded, but assures them of a hundredfold now and eternal life to come.
29:
"And Jesus answered and said, Verily I say unto you, There is no man that hath left house, or brethren, or sisters, or father, or mother, or wife, or children, or lands, for my sake, and the gospel's." The witnesses note Christ's generous answer and Mark's distinctive phrasing. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that "St. Mark, possibly as writing for Gentile converts, omits the special promise to the Twelve, that they should sit on thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel" (Matthew 19:28) — a promise less apt for a Gentile readership. Clarke notes the strong textual support for "for the sake of the gospel," and Gill marks the same omission of the thrones promise.

The key phrase, the expositors agree, is "for my sake, and the gospel's." Theophylact (on v.28) had been careful to guard it: Christ "does not mean that we should leave our fathers without helping them, or separate ourselves from our wives, but instructs us to prefer the glory of God to the things of this world." The leaving in view is not the neglect of natural duty but the readiness to part with all earthly ties when they stand between the soul and Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the charter of the disciple's reward. Christ promises that no sacrifice made "for my sake, and the gospel's" shall go unrequited; whatever a man surrenders — home, kindred, lands — for the sake of Christ and his good news, he shall receive again a hundredfold. The lesson the expositors press is the breadth and the condition of the promise. Its breadth: it embraces every earthly tie a man might forsake. Its condition: the leaving must be "for my sake, and the gospel's" — not a careless abandonment of duty, but the deliberate preferring of Christ above all. Mark's addition "and the gospel's," paralleling his addition at the saying about losing one's life, the witnesses note, binds the cause of Christ inseparably to the cause of the gospel, so that to forsake all for the spreading of the good news is to forsake all for Christ himself. And Mark's omission of the thrones-promise, fitted to his Gentile readers, leaves the universal promise standing: that the Lord rewards every sacrifice made for him, in this life and the next.
30:
"But he shall receive an hundredfold now in this time, houses, and brethren, and sisters, and mothers, and children, and lands, with persecutions; and in the world to come eternal life." The witnesses expound the hundredfold reward, its present form, and the persecutions that attend it. The grammatical-historical Barnes insists it "cannot be taken literally, as promising a hundred times as many mothers, sisters," but means "that the loss shall be a hundred times compensated; or that, in the possession of religion, we have a hundred times the value of all that we forsake" — "the pardon of sin, the favour of God, peace of conscience, support in trials, and friends raised up in the place of those left, spiritual brethren and sisters and mothers." Theophylact (on v.28) had given the same: Peter, "leaving one house, had afterwards the houses of all the disciples."

The witnesses stress the words "with persecutions," peculiar to Mark. Barnes: "persecutions, or the contempt of the world, they must meet; Jesus did not conceal this. But he consoled them" — "it is well to bear trial if God be our friend." The reward is real but not exempt from suffering; the saints "possess all things with persecutions" (Theophylact). Bede (on v.28) records, and rejects, the millenarian misuse of "hundredfold now": some drew from it "that Jewish fable of a thousand years after the resurrection, when all we have left is to be restored with manifold usury."

So the witnesses make the verse the full measure of the disciple's recompense, in this life and the next. The hundredfold "now" is not a literal multiplying of houses and kindred but the immeasurable gain of the spiritual life — the favour of God, the fellowship of the saints, peace, and provision — which a hundred times outweighs all that is forsaken; yet it comes "with persecutions," so that the reward is no exemption from the cross but its companion. The lesson the expositors draw is the honesty and the generosity of Christ's promise: he hides not the suffering, naming the persecutions plainly, yet promises that even amid them the disciple shall find a hundredfold compensation, and beyond them, "in the world to come, eternal life." The present reward, with its persecutions, and the future reward of eternal life together answer Peter's question and overturn the rich young man's choice; for what he would not surrender his goods to gain, the disciples, surrendering their little all, receive a hundredfold and an eternity besides.
31:
"But many that are first shall be last; and the last first." The witnesses read this paradox as a gentle check upon the disciples' expectation of reward. The grammatical-historical Robertson takes it as "probably a rebuke here to Peter's boast" — a caution against presuming on the order of reward, lest those who reckon themselves first by their sacrifice be found last by their self-regard. Ellicott notes that "St. Mark omits the parable of the Labourers in the Vineyard, which follows in St. Matthew as an illustration of the truth" — the parable that teaches the same lesson at length.

Theophylact (on v.28) had applied the saying historically: "the Pharisees who were first became the last; but those who left all and followed Christ were last in this world through tribulation and persecutions, but shall be first by the hope which is in God." The reversal works both ways — the proud first made last, the suffering last made first.

So the witnesses make the verse the qualification of the great promise. Lest the disciples, hearing of the hundredfold reward, fall into the very pride that ruined the Pharisees, Christ adds this warning: that the order of the kingdom is not the order of human reckoning, and that many who count themselves first may be found last. The lesson the expositors draw is humility in the midst of reward. The promise of recompense is sure, but it is not to be claimed in a spirit of self-congratulation; the disciple who serves with an eye to his own precedence, as Peter's question faintly betrayed, may find the places reversed. Mark's placing of the saying immediately after the promise, without the vineyard parable Matthew adds, the witnesses note, makes it the sharper: the same breath that promises the hundredfold warns against presuming upon it. The first-and-last reversal thus stands as the kingdom's standing rebuke to spiritual pride and its standing comfort to humble suffering — abasing those who exalt themselves in their service, and exalting those who, last and lowly here, trust their reward to the hope that is in God. The verse, brief as it is, thus gathers up the whole exchange with Peter into a single principle: that grace rewards but does not pander to ambition, and that the surest way to be last in the kingdom is to count oneself first.
32:
"And they were in the way going up to Jerusalem; and Jesus went before them: and they were amazed; and as they followed, they were afraid. And he took again the twelve, and began to tell them what things should happen unto him." The witnesses dwell on the awe of the disciples and the resolve of Christ. The Latin Bede reads their amazement as foreboding: remembering that he had "foretold that he was about to suffer many things," they were "amazed, lest they themselves should perish with him, or lest he, whose life was their joy, should fall under his enemies." Theophylact draws out the meaning of Christ going before: "to shew that he runs to meet his Passion, and does not refuse death for our salvation"; and he foretells it "that from hearing these things beforehand they might the better bear them afterwards, and might not be alarmed at their suddenness" — and "to shew that he suffered voluntarily, for he who foreknows a danger and flies not, though flight is in his power, of his own will gives himself up to suffering."

Theophylact notes Christ "takes his disciples apart, because it was fitting that he should reveal the mystery of his Passion to those more closely connected with him," and "enumerates each thing that was to happen, lest if he should pass anything over, they should be troubled at suddenly seeing it." Chrysostom adds that he joins the resurrection to the passion, "that when they were saddened on account of his death, they should then also look for his Resurrection."

So the witnesses make the verse a vivid picture of the road to the cross. Christ strides ahead, his face set toward Jerusalem and the suffering he has thrice foretold; the disciples follow amazed and afraid, sensing the gathering doom yet not comprehending it. The lesson the expositors draw is the voluntariness and the foreknowledge of the Saviour's passion. He does not stumble unawares into death but goes before, running to meet it, choosing it freely for the salvation of men; and he forewarns his own, item by item, both that they might not be overwhelmed by its suddenness and that they might know it was no defeat but the appointed work he willingly embraced. The amazement and fear of the disciples, the witnesses note, only set off the calm resolve of Christ, who leads the way to his own cross with a steadiness that proves his sacrifice was not wrung from him but offered — the willing self-giving of the One who came to give his life a ransom for many.
33:
"Saying, Behold, we go up to Jerusalem; and the Son of man shall be delivered unto the chief priests, and unto the scribes; and they shall condemn him to death, and shall deliver him to the Gentiles." The witnesses note the precision of this third and fullest passion prediction.
