The Latin Gloss parses each clause: "the Son of man, that is, He to whom suffering belongs, for the Godhead cannot suffer; shall be delivered, that is, by Judas, unto the chief priests and scribes, and they shall condemn him to death, judging him guilty of death; and shall deliver him to the Gentiles, that is, to Pilate the Gentile." Each agent and each stage is named.

Cyril of Jerusalem draws out the willingness again: "wouldest thou be persuaded that he came to his passion willingly? others, who foreknow it not, die unwillingly; but he spoke before of his passion, Behold, we go up to Jerusalem, and the Son of man shall be betrayed and shall be crucified... It was lest the whole world should perish in its sins." Gill notes simply that "they were now upon the road thither," the prediction given as they walked toward its fulfilment.

So the witnesses make the verse the most detailed of Christ's foretellings of his death, naming the very course of his passion before it came to pass. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, with the Gloss, the careful distinction of natures: it is "the Son of man" who suffers, for "the Godhead cannot suffer" — yet it is the one Christ, God and man, who goes to the cross, the impassible Deity united to the passible flesh. Second, with Cyril, the willing foreknowledge of the Saviour: he traces in advance the whole path — betrayal by Judas, condemnation by the Jewish council, delivery to the Gentile Pilate — proving that he went to death not as one overtaken but as one who chose it, "lest the whole world should perish in its sins." The precision of the prediction, the witnesses note, would afterward be its own confirmation: when each detail came to pass exactly as foretold, the disciples would know that their Master had not been defeated by his enemies but had laid down his life by his own foreknowing will, the appointed Lamb going up to Jerusalem to be slain.
34:
"And they shall mock him, and shall scourge him, and shall spit upon him, and shall kill him: and the third day he shall rise again." The witnesses note the catalogue of indignities and a detail Mark shares with Luke. The grammatical-historical Ellicott observes that "shall spit upon him" is given "in common with St. Luke, but not with St. Matthew" — a stroke of the foretold humiliation peculiar to the two. Barnes cross-references the prophetic portrait of the mocked and reproached Sufferer (Psalm 22:6-7, 13). Gill notes that "the Gentiles, as the Roman soldiers did," would carry out the mockery and scourging, "the one at the connivance, and the other by the order of their governor."

The prediction descends to the very particulars of the passion — mocking, scourging, spitting, killing — and crowns them with the resurrection: "the third day he shall rise again." The witnesses note that Christ never foretells the cross without the rising; the dreadful catalogue ends in triumph.

So the witnesses make the verse the completion of the third passion prediction, sparing the disciples nothing of the shame to come yet joining to it the promise of victory. The lesson the expositors draw is the exactness of Christ's foreknowledge and the constancy of his hope. He foretells not only that he will die but how — the mockery, the scourge, the spitting, the killing — each of which the Gospels will record as fulfilled to the letter, so that the prophecy becomes the proof that his death was foreseen and chosen, not suffered by surprise. And he foretells, as always, the resurrection: "the third day he shall rise again." The disciples, the witnesses note (with Chrysostom on v.32), could not yet take in this last word, fixed in dread upon the killing; yet Christ joins it to the rest, that when the shame came it might not extinguish their faith, the One who foretold his death with such precision having also foretold his rising. The catalogue of indignities, ending in resurrection, thus holds together the whole mystery the disciples were so slow to grasp — that the path of mockery and the cross was the path to the third day's glory.
35:
"And James and John, the sons of Zebedee, come unto him, saying, Master, we would that thou shouldest do for us whatsoever we shall desire." The witnesses expound the ambitious request and note Mark's distinctive form. The Antiochene Chrysostom explains the motive: "the disciples, hearing Christ often speaking of his kingdom, thought this kingdom was to be before his death; and therefore now that his death was foretold, they came that they might immediately be made worthy of the honours of the kingdom." He notes their shame — "ashamed of the human weakness they felt, they came taking him apart from the disciples" — and that Christ, "not from ignorance of what they wanted, but from a wish of making them answer, puts the question, What would ye?"

The grammatical-historical commentators note the difference from Matthew: there the request comes through the mother (Matthew 20:20); here the brothers ask directly. Chrysostom harmonises: "both took place; that they might the more easily succeed, they took their mother with them, that they might pray together with her." Robertson (on v.37) marks the worldliness of their dream: "they are looking for a grand Jewish world empire... that dream brushed aside all the talk of Jesus about his death and resurrection as mere pessimism."

So the witnesses make the verse a striking instance of the disciples' carnal misunderstanding, set immediately after the third prediction of the cross. The Lord has just foretold his mockery, scourging, and death; and James and John, hearing it as the prelude to an earthly kingdom soon to come, hasten to secure for themselves the chief places. The lesson the expositors draw is the depth of the disciples' blindness and the patience of Christ. They could not yet conceive of a kingdom won through suffering, and so, deaf to the passion just foretold, they grasped at glory; their request — a blank petition, "do for us whatsoever we shall desire" — betrays at once their ambition and their presumption. Yet Christ does not crush them, but draws out their meaning with a question, that he may correct their ignorance and turn their eyes from the thrones they coveted to the cup they must drink. The contrast the witnesses feel is sharp: the Master speaking of his death, the disciples scheming for precedence — a contrast Christ will resolve by teaching that the way to glory in his kingdom is not the seat of honour but the cup of suffering and the place of a servant.
36:
"And he said unto them, What would ye that I should do for you?" The witnesses read Christ's counter-question as a method of teaching, drawing the ambitious request into the open. Gill notes the question was put "also to their mother, and to them by her," gathering the whole petition — the brothers' and the mother's together — into one. The Lord does not refuse outright, nor grant blindly, but asks what they would have, that they may name it themselves.

Chrysostom (on v.35) had explained the purpose: Christ asks "not from ignorance of what they wanted to ask, but from a wish of making them answer him." By requiring them to put their desire into words, he exposes its carnality to themselves, and prepares the correction that follows. The question is the gentle instrument of a teacher who would have his pupils see their own error.

So the witnesses make the brief verse a window into the wisdom of Christ's dealing with mistaken desire. He might have rebuked the request unspoken, or silenced it; instead he invites it — "What would ye that I should do for you?" — that the brothers, hearing their own ambition named aloud, might feel its unfitness, and that he might answer it not with a flat denial but with instruction. The lesson the expositors draw is the patience and the method of the Saviour, who deals with his disciples' ignorance not by crushing it but by drawing it out and correcting it. The same question, the witnesses note, that Christ here asks of James and John — "What would ye?" — he asks, in effect, of every disciple who comes to him with a request, that the desire might be brought into the light of his presence and there examined, purified, or, where it is amiss, gently redirected. By requiring them to speak their petition, Christ opens the way for the teaching about the cup and the servant that will turn their grasping after thrones into the readiness to suffer and to serve. The brief verse, then, is no mere transition but a small lesson in itself, showing how Christ turns even a presumptuous request into an occasion of instruction, by bringing the hidden desire into the open where it can be examined and, in love, corrected.
37:
"They said unto him, Grant unto us that we may sit, one on thy right hand, and the other on thy left hand, in thy glory." The witnesses note the substance of the request and Mark's distinctive wording. The grammatical-historical Robertson observes that "Mark has 'in thy glory' where Matthew has 'in thy kingdom'" — both expressing the same expectation: "they are looking for a grand Jewish world empire with apocalyptic features in the eschatological culmination of the Messiah's kingdom; that dream brushed aside all the talk of Jesus about his death and resurrection as mere pessimism." Clarke notes a reading "in the kingdom of thy glory," "which kingdom they expected to be established on earth." Gill records that they asked "by their mother, or seconding her motion."

The request, the expositors agree, is for the two chief places in the coming kingdom — the seats at the right and left hand of the enthroned Messiah, the positions of highest honour and nearest authority. It is the natural petition of men who imagined the kingdom to be an earthly empire soon to be set up, in which the disciples would reign as the king's chief ministers.

So the witnesses make the verse the precise statement of the brothers' ambition, set against the cross just foretold. They ask to sit at Christ's right and left "in his glory," conceiving that glory as an earthly throne and themselves as its chief courtiers. The lesson the expositors draw is the gulf between the disciples' expectation and the truth. Christ had spoken of being mocked, scourged, and killed; James and John heard only "kingdom" and "glory," and hastened to claim the best places in it. Their request is not wicked — they do love and trust their Master, and believe in his coming reign — but it is carnal, measuring his glory by earthly thrones and seeking precedence rather than service. Mark's "in thy glory," the witnesses note, may even sharpen the irony: for the glory of Christ would first be revealed not on a throne but on a cross, where the places at his right and left hand would be filled, not by his honoured disciples, but by two crucified robbers. The brothers ask for seats of honour in a kingdom whose King was going to his death; and Christ will answer by pointing them, not to the throne, but to the cup he must drink.
38:
"But Jesus said unto them, Ye know not what ye ask: can ye drink of the cup that I drink of? and be baptized with the baptism that I am baptized with?" The witnesses read Christ's reply as the turning of ambition toward suffering, and note Mark's double figure. The Antiochene Chrysostom paraphrases: "Ye know not what ye ask; as if he said, Ye speak of honours, but I am discoursing of wrestlings and toil; for this is not a time of rewards, but of blood, of battles, and dangers." The grammatical-historical Robertson notes that "Matthew has only the cup, but Mark has both the cup and the baptism, both referring to death" — Christ would call his death "the cup" again in Gethsemane (Mark 14:36) and had used "baptism" for it (Luke 12:50); Ellicott observes the baptism-clause "seems to have been found originally in St. Mark only."

The Wesleyan Wesley distinguishes the two images: "the cup of inward, the baptism of outward sufferings; our Lord was filled with sufferings within, and covered with them without." Cyril of Jerusalem connects both to martyrdom: "for martyrdom the Saviour is wont to call a baptism, saying, Can ye drink the cup which I drink, and be baptized with the baptism that I am baptized with?"

So the witnesses make the verse the great redirection of the brothers' desire. They had asked for thrones; Christ answers with a cup and a baptism — the figures of his own approaching death. The lesson the expositors draw is that the way to glory in Christ's kingdom lies through suffering, not around it. "Ye know not what ye ask," he tells them: they sought the seats of honour without reckoning the cost, ignorant that the place at his right and left would be won, not by precedence, but by sharing his passion. The two figures, the witnesses note (the cup and the baptism, both peculiar in their fulness to Mark), deepen the lesson: suffering inward and outward, a draught to be drunk and a flood to be plunged into, both pointing to death. Christ does not yet deny the brothers' request; he questions whether they can bear what it truly entails. He turns their eyes from the throne to the cross, teaching that those who would sit with him in glory must first drink with him the cup of suffering — a lesson the Church would learn in the blood of its martyrs, and a baptism, as Cyril says, that even without water receives the kingdom.
39:
"And they said unto him, We can. And Jesus said unto them, Ye shall indeed drink of the cup that I drink of; and with the baptism that I am baptized withal shall ye be baptized." The witnesses note the brothers' confident answer, its ignorance, and Christ's solemn promise. The Reformed Gill marks the self-ignorance: "we can — which is another instance of their ignorance; for as they knew not the glorious state they desired places in, so they were unacquainted with themselves, ignorant of their own weakness as well as of the greatness of the sufferings"; had they known either, "they would not have expressed themselves in this manner without any mention of the grace of God, or any dependence on the strength of Christ."

Yet Christ takes their bold "We can" and turns it into a true prophecy. The grammatical-historical commentators note that the promise was fulfilled — James was the first apostle martyred (Acts 12:2), and John drank deep of suffering for Christ. Chrysostom (on v.35) had read it so: "ye shall be worthy of martyrdom, and suffer even as I." Cyril again connects the cup and baptism to the martyr's confession.

So the witnesses make the verse the meeting of presumption and promise. The brothers, ignorant alike of the kingdom they coveted and the weakness within them, answer "We can" without a thought of their need of grace; and Christ, instead of exposing their rashness, accepts the word and makes it a prophecy — they shall indeed drink his cup and share his baptism. The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the danger of self-confidence: the disciples spoke of bearing Christ's sufferings as if by their own strength, "without any dependence on the strength of Christ," a presumption that the hour of trial would soon humble. Second, the grace that fulfils even a rash vow: Christ does not hold their boast against them but, by his grace, brings it to pass, so that James and John, who claimed they could suffer with him, were indeed given to suffer for him. The promise, the witnesses note, answers their request more truly than they knew: they had asked for places at his right and left in glory, and Christ grants them, not the thrones, but the cup — the fellowship of his sufferings, which is the only way to the fellowship of his glory.
40:
"But to sit on my right hand and on my left hand is not mine to give; but it shall be given to them for whom it is prepared." The witnesses confront the hard saying and guard it from misreading. The grammatical-historical Ellicott corrects the text and the sense: "omitting the interpolated words, it is not Mine to give, but to those for whom it has been prepared. Our Lord disclaims, not the power to give, but that of giving arbitrarily, otherwise than His Father willed." Chrysostom (on v.35) had answered the difficulty at length: Christ "answers the thoughts of those who asked him, condescending to their meaning," for they sought only "the chief place, to be set over others," not knowing the true throne; the seats are not given by favour but to those fitted for them.

The Wesleyan Wesley names who they are: the places are "for them who by patient continuance in well doing seek for glory, and honour, and immortality; for these only eternal life is prepared." Barnes cross-references "the kingdom prepared for you from the foundation of the world" (Matthew 25:34). The reward is not dispensed by arbitrary preference but bestowed according to the Father's appointment, on those prepared for it by grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the correction of the brothers' whole presumption. They had sought the chief places as a personal favour, to be granted at the asking; Christ answers that such places are "not mine to give" in that manner — not because he lacks the power, the expositors are careful to say, but because they are bestowed not by partiality but according to the Father's preparation, "to them for whom it is prepared." The lesson the expositors press is that the honours of the kingdom are not the spoils of ambition nor the gifts of favouritism, but the appointed reward of those whom God has fitted for them by patient well-doing and the fellowship of Christ's sufferings. To ask for the seats of glory as James and John asked is to mistake the kingdom utterly; the way to those seats lies not through petition for precedence but through the cup of suffering and the path of service that Christ will now set before all the Twelve. The verse thus closes the brothers' request as it began — gently, without crushing, yet turning their grasping after honour into the sober knowledge that the places of glory are prepared by the Father for those made ready for them, and won by no other way than the cross.
41:
"And when the ten heard it, they began to be much displeased with James and John." The witnesses read the indignation of the ten as itself a symptom of the same ambition. The Antiochene Theophylact diagnoses it: "the other Apostles are indignant at seeing James and John seeking for honour; for, being influenced by human feelings, they were moved with envy; and their first displeasure arose from seeing that they were not taken up by the Lord. Before that time they were not displeased, because they saw that they themselves were honoured before other men. At this time the Apostles were thus imperfect, but afterwards they yielded the chief place one to another."

The ten, that is, were displeased not from a higher principle but from the same desire of precedence: they resented the two brothers' bid for the chief places because they wanted those places themselves. The whole company, the witnesses note, was infected with the spirit of ambition, the two who asked and the ten who were angry alike.

So the witnesses make the verse the occasion of Christ's great teaching on greatness. The brothers' request had stirred up envy among the rest, exposing that the carnal hope of an earthly kingdom and its honours was not confined to James and John but ran through the Twelve. The lesson the expositors draw is the universality of the disease and the gentleness of the Physician. Theophylact's observation is searching: the ten had not been troubled before, while they each thought themselves honoured; their displeasure flared only when two reached for what all secretly desired. Yet Christ, the witnesses note, does not take sides in the quarrel, condemning the two or the ten, but calls them all to himself to heal the common fault. The indignation of the ten, far from being righteous zeal, was envy; and Christ meets it not by adjudicating who deserves the highest place, but by overturning the whole notion of greatness on which the dispute rested — teaching that in his kingdom the way up is down, and the greatest is the servant of all. The verse thus sets the stage for the lesson that follows, in which the ambition of the two and the envy of the ten are alike answered by the example of the Son of man, who came not to be served but to serve.
42:
"But Jesus called them to him, and saith unto them, Ye know that they which are accounted to rule over the Gentiles exercise lordship over them; and their great ones exercise authority upon them." The witnesses read this as Christ's diagnosis of worldly greatness, set over against the greatness of his kingdom. The Antiochene Theophylact notes the method: Christ "cures them, first by drawing them to himself in order to comfort them — this is meant when it says, But Jesus called them to him — then by shewing them that to usurp honour, and to desire the chief place, belongs to Gentiles." The "great ones of the Gentiles thrust themselves into the chief place tyrannically and as lords."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the force of the two verbs, "exercise lordship" and "exercise authority" — both describing the domineering rule of earthly princes, who hold power as a personal possession and lord it over their subjects. This, Christ says, is the pattern of the world; and it is precisely the pattern the disciples had been following in their strife for precedence.

So the witnesses make the verse the foil against which Christ defines true greatness. He first calls the quarrelling disciples to himself — a gesture of healing, drawing them near to comfort and instruct — and then holds up before them the way of the world: among the Gentiles, the rulers domineer, and the great ones lord it over the rest. The lesson the expositors draw is that the disciples, in their grasping after the chief places, were thinking and acting as heathen princes, measuring greatness by authority over others. Christ names this worldly pattern plainly, that they may see how unfit it is for his kingdom. The point, the witnesses note, is not merely that earthly rulers happen to behave so, but that this — the exercise of lordship, the love of precedence and power — is the very essence of worldly greatness, and the opposite of the greatness Christ will commend. By setting the Gentile way first, he prepares the great reversal of the next verse: "but so shall it not be among you." The disciples are to take their pattern not from the princes of the Gentiles, who rule and domineer, but from their Master, who came to serve — and the verse that exposes the world's idea of greatness clears the ground for the kingdom's.
43:
"But so shall it not be among you: but whosoever will be great among you, shall be your minister." The witnesses read this as the great reversal that defines greatness in Christ's kingdom. The Reformed Gill notes the force of the prohibition: "so shall it not be among you — or, so let it not be; it ought not to be so among you; such a lordly spirit does not become you; this is heathenish." The pattern of the Gentile princes, just described, is forbidden to the disciples; theirs is to be the opposite way.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks a precise variation: in both this verse and the next, the Greek is "shall be your minister," "shall be servant," not "let him be" — a statement of fact rather than mere command, as though to say that this is simply how greatness works in the kingdom. Whoever truly would be great among Christ's people will find that the path is service: the greatness is not seized by ruling but attained by ministering.

So the witnesses make the verse the overturning of all worldly ambition. Where the Gentiles count greatness as lordship over others, Christ declares that among his disciples greatness is service to others: "whosoever will be great among you, shall be your minister." The lesson the expositors draw is the complete inversion of the world's order. The disciples had been striving, like heathen princes, for the chief seats and the authority that goes with them; Christ tells them that in his kingdom the way to be great is to serve, and the higher a man would rise, the lower he must stoop. The verb Ellicott notes — "shall be," not "let him be" — gives the teaching a deeper edge: it is not only a rule to be obeyed but a truth to be recognized, that real greatness in the things of God is constituted by lowly service, so that the servant is, in fact, the great one. This is not the abolition of greatness but its redefinition: there is a true greatness to be sought among Christ's people, but it is found by the road the world despises — the road of ministering to others, on which the disciple follows his Master, who is about to name himself the servant of all.
44:
"And whosoever of you will be the chiefest, shall be servant of all." The witnesses read this as the deepening of the reversal: not only the great but the chiefest must be the lowest. Gill, who carries the comment, paraphrases the aim: he who "is desirous of the highest place and the greatest honour" must take the lowest place and serve all. The teaching mounts from the previous verse — the great one is the minister; the chiefest is the "servant of all."

The expositors note the intensification. In v.43 the one who would be great is to be a "minister" (diakonos); here the one who would be first, the "chiefest," is to be "servant of all" — the stronger word (doulos, bondservant), and the wider range, "of all." The higher the place desired, the lower the service required; the man who would be first of all must be the slave of all.

So the witnesses make the verse the climax of Christ's teaching on greatness, completing the inversion of worldly ambition. To be "chiefest" in the kingdom is not to rule over the most but to serve the most — to be, not the master of all, but the "servant of all." The lesson the expositors draw is the thoroughness of the reversal. The world's first is he who commands the many; Christ's first is he who serves the many, sinking lowest in self-abasement that he may minister most widely. This is the very opposite of the disciples' dispute, in which each sought to be set over the rest; Christ tells them that the way to the highest place is to become the bondservant of all, refusing precedence and embracing the lowest ministry. The teaching, the witnesses note, is not a counsel of mere modesty but the law of the kingdom, grounded in the next verse on the example of the Son of man himself, who, being chiefest of all, became servant of all, and gave his life for them. The disciple who would be first, then, is pointed not to a throne but to a towel and a cross — to the place of the slave, where alone, in Christ's kingdom, true preeminence is found.
45:
"For even the Son of man came not to be ministered unto, but to minister, and to give his life a ransom for many." The witnesses receive this as the ground of the whole teaching and one of the great statements of the atonement. The Latin Bede sets it as the crowning example: Christ "proposes an example, that if they lightly regarded his words, his deeds might make them ashamed" — and, crucially, "he did not say that he gave his life a ransom for all, but for many, that is, for those who would believe on him." The grammatical-historical Robertson dwells on the ransom: "in verse 45 note the language of Jesus concerning his death as 'a ransom for many' (lytron anti pollōn), words of the Master not understood by the apostles when spoken, preserved for us by Peter through Mark"; and he warns against those "who seek to empty these words of all real meaning, as if Jesus could not have such a conception concerning his death for sinners."

Barnes gathers the prophetic and apostolic witness to the substitutionary death — Christ "made sin for us" (2 Corinthians 5:21), "made a curse for us" (Galatians 3:13), "a ransom for all" (1 Timothy 2:6) — and Wesley glosses "a ransom for many" as "even for as many souls as needed such a ransom" (2 Corinthians 5:15). Theophylact (on v.41) had marvelled at the condescension: "what can be greater than that a man should die for him to whom he ministers?"

So the witnesses make the verse the foundation of the lesson on greatness and the heart of the gospel. Christ enforces his teaching that the chiefest must be servant of all by his own supreme example: the Son of man, greatest of all, "came not to be ministered unto, but to minister" — and his ministry reached its height in giving his life "a ransom for many." The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. For greatness: the disciples are to seek the lowest service because their Master, infinitely above them, took the lowest place, serving even to death. For salvation: the death of Christ was no mere martyrdom but a ransom — a price (lytron) paid in the place of (anti) the many, to redeem them from bondage; Robertson insists these words bear their full weight, the considered teaching of Christ about his atoning death. Bede's note that it was "for many, not all... for those who would believe" the witnesses record as marking the efficacy of the ransom for the redeemed; Wesley and others read "many" of all who need it. Either way, the verse joins the example of service to the work of redemption: the servant of all is the Saviour of many, and the same self-giving that teaches the disciples humility accomplishes the world's salvation.
46:
"And they came to Jericho: and as he went out of Jericho with his disciples and a great number of people, blind Bartimaeus, the son of Timaeus, sat by the highway side begging." The witnesses introduce the last miracle before the Passion and address a notable harmonising question. The Latin Bede confronts the difference among the Gospels: "Matthew says there were two blind men; Luke relates that one was enlightened as he was going into Jericho. No wise man will suppose the Evangelists wrote things contrary to one another, but that one wrote more fully what another left out." He concludes "one of them was the more important, which appears from this, that Mark has related his name and his father's name."

Bede draws out an elaborate allegory: Jericho "means moon, and points out the waning of our fleeting race"; Christ restored sight "drawing near to his Passion," for "it was not in the first years of his Incarnation, but in the few before he suffered, that he shewed the mystery of the Word." And the number — one healed leaving, two entering (Matthew) — he reads of the gospel going first to one nation, the Jews, then through the apostles to "both Jews and Gentiles." Jerome adds that "Jericho means moon or anathema; but the failing of the flesh of Christ is the preparation of the heavenly Jerusalem."

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of the Bartimaeus miracle and a case-study in Gospel harmony. The lesson the expositors draw, with Bede, is that the apparent discrepancies — two blind men or one, entering Jericho or leaving — are not contradictions but the natural variety of independent witnesses, one recording more fully than another; and Mark's distinctive preservation of the name, "Bartimaeus, the son of Timaeus," marks the man as the more notable of the two, known by name in the early Church. The setting too is significant: Christ heals the blind beggar as he passes through Jericho on the way up to Jerusalem and the cross — the last of his recorded healings before the Passion, given (as Bede reads it) at the very threshold of his suffering, that the opening of blind eyes might be a sign of the greater light he was going to win. The blind man by the wayside, helpless and begging, is set in the witnesses' reading as the type of fallen man, who must cry to the passing Christ for the light he cannot give himself.
47:
"And when he heard that it was Jesus of Nazareth, he began to cry out, and say, Jesus, thou Son of David, have mercy on me." The witnesses note the beggar's discovery of who passed by and his confession of faith in the cry "Son of David." Gill describes how he came to know: hearing "such a multitude of people, and the noise," he "might learn, by inquiring the meaning, or hear his name frequently mentioned, and it said that he was coming, or passing by."

The cry "Son of David," the expositors agree, is a confession of Jesus as the Messiah; for "Son of David" was the recognised Messianic title, and the blind beggar, who could not see, yet saw by faith what the seeing crowd did not — that the passing Nazarene was the promised Christ. His petition, "have mercy on me," is the cry of helpless need cast upon the Messiah's compassion.

So the witnesses make the verse the beginning of Bartimaeus's bold and believing appeal. Hearing that Jesus of Nazareth was passing, he seizes the one chance he may never have again, and cries aloud — not merely to a wonder-worker, but to the "Son of David," the Messiah, owning by that title a faith the multitude lacked. The lesson the expositors draw, here and in the verses that follow, is the earnestness and the discernment of faith. The blind man's bodily darkness had not darkened his soul; he perceived in Jesus the Son of David, and would not let the moment pass without crying for mercy. His confession, the witnesses note, is the more striking for its setting: the seeing crowd thronged Christ without owning him Messiah, while the blind beggar by the road confessed him aloud. And his cry is the model of every sinner's approach — a confession of who Christ is joined to a plea for mercy from one who has nothing to offer and everything to ask. The beggar who could give nothing could yet cry; and his cry, despised by the crowd, would be heard by the Son of David, who never turned away the appeal of faith.
48:
"And many charged him that he should hold his peace: but he cried the more a great deal, Thou Son of David, have mercy on me." The witnesses note the opposition of the crowd and the beggar's redoubled persistence. The grammatical-historical Robertson draws out the tenses: "rebuked him (imperfect) — kept rebuking repeatedly," and "should hold his peace (ingressive aorist) — become silent"; yet he cried "the more a great deal." Gill notes they charged him "to be silent, and not call him the Son of David, at least, nor ask anything of him." Barnes cross-references the promise to those who seek with the whole heart (Jeremiah 29:13) and the God who renders mercy (Psalm 62:12).

The crowd's rebuke, the expositors agree, only quickened the beggar's faith. Where many would have been silenced by the discouragement, Bartimaeus "cried the more a great deal," refusing to be hindered from the one Saviour who could help him. His persistence in the face of opposition is the mark of an earnest and importunate faith.

So the witnesses make the verse a study in the perseverance of faith against discouragement. The very crowd that followed Christ tried to silence the man who cried to him; but their rebuke, far from quenching his appeal, inflamed it, so that "he cried the more a great deal." The lesson the expositors draw is that true faith is not put off by obstacles but presses through them. The opposition Bartimaeus met — the impatience of the multitude, who thought the blind beggar beneath the Master's notice — is the kind of hindrance every seeking soul encounters, whether from the world's contempt or its own fears; and his response is the pattern of importunate prayer, which, the more it is repulsed, the more earnestly it cries. The God who, as Barnes notes, is found of those who seek him "with all the heart" (Jeremiah 29:13) honours such persistence; and Bartimaeus, crying louder against the crowd's rebuke, would be heard where a fainter faith would have fallen silent and lost its blessing. The verse thus commends the holy stubbornness of faith, which will take no denial and will not be silenced till it has reached the ear of the Son of David.
49:
"And Jesus stood still, and commanded him to be called. And they call the blind man, saying unto him, Be of good comfort, rise; he calleth thee." The witnesses dwell on Christ's stopping and the sudden change in the crowd. The grammatical-historical Robertson notes "Jesus stood still" (ingressive aorist) — the Lord halts his procession to Jerusalem for one blind beggar — and that "he calleth thee" was "joyful news to Bartimaeus," with "vivid dramatic presents here in Mark." Ellicott marks that the better text gives "more vividly, and said, Call him," and that "the cheering words of the disciples or bystanders are given by St. Mark only" — "Be of good comfort."

The expositors note the reversal in the multitude. The very people who had charged the beggar to be silent now turn to encourage him: "Be of good comfort, rise; he calleth thee." The word of Christ changes their tone in a moment; what they had hindered, at his call they now further.

So the witnesses make the verse a picture of Christ's response to persistent faith and its effect on others. The Lord, on his way to the cross, "stood still" at the cry of one blind beggar — a small detail, the witnesses note, of immense tenderness, that the Saviour of the world should halt his solemn journey for the sake of a roadside outcast whom the crowd despised. And his call transformed the crowd: those who had rebuked Bartimaeus now cheer him, "Be of good comfort... he calleth thee." The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the readiness of Christ to hear the importunate: he is never too pressed, even on the way to his Passion, to stop for the cry of faith. Second, the change his favour works in the bystanders: once Christ owns the seeker, the world's rebuke turns to encouragement. Mark's tender details, preserved (as the tradition holds) through Peter — the standing still, the bystanders' "Be of good comfort," the joyful "he calleth thee" — the witnesses note, fill the scene with the warmth of an eyewitness memory, and make the moment a model of the gospel call itself: the persistent cry of the helpless, the gracious halting of Christ, and the summons "he calleth thee," which turns despair to good comfort.
50:
"And he, casting away his garment, rose, and came to Jesus." The witnesses dwell on the beggar's eager haste and find in it a parable of the sinner's coming to Christ. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes the vividness of Mark's detail, "the eager action of the man casting off his garment and leaping up," the Greek "much stronger than the English rose." Barnes and Clarke explain the garment as "his outer mantle," the "loose upper garment of an Asiatic mendicant," which he "threw off, full of joy at the prospect of being healed, that he might run without impediment to Jesus."

Both Barnes and Clarke draw the application. Barnes: "this may be used to illustrate the manner in which a sinner should come to Jesus — he should throw away the garments of his own righteousness, rise speedily, run with joy, and cast himself entirely upon his mercy." Clarke adds the memorable anecdote of the converted negro, who said the white man was slow to find salvation because "you white men have much clothing upon you, and when Christ calls you cannot run to him; but we poor negroes have only this, and when we hear the call, we throw it off instantly, and run" — illustrating the text "as well as any doctor in the universe."

So the witnesses make the verse a picture of the wholehearted response of faith to Christ's call. At the word "he calleth thee," Bartimaeus does not hesitate: he flings off his cloak, springs up, and comes. The lesson the expositors draw, here turned to spiritual use, is the readiness with which the seeking soul should answer Christ. The blind man cast away the one garment that might have hindered his haste; so the sinner, called by Christ, must cast away whatever would impede his coming — above all "the garments of his own righteousness," in which men so often wrap themselves and so cannot run to the Saviour. Bartimaeus's eager, unimpeded rush to Jesus, the witnesses note, is the very opposite of the rich young man earlier in the chapter, who "went away sorrowful" because he would not part with his goods: the beggar threw off his one poor cloak and came; the rich man clutched his great possessions and turned away. The lesson is that nothing must be allowed to come between the called soul and the calling Christ; and the man who, like Bartimaeus, casts off every encumbrance and runs at the first word, will find, as he found, that Christ was waiting to make him whole.
51:
"And Jesus answered and said unto him, What wilt thou that I should do unto thee? The blind man said unto him, Lord, that I might receive my sight." The witnesses note Christ's question and the beggar's reverent answer, and they dwell on Mark's preservation of the very word he spoke. The grammatical-historical Ellicott marks it: the word rendered "Lord" is better "Rabboni, the same as in John 20:16, occurring in these two passages only" — "an augmentative form of Rabbi, expressing greater reverence," and "another example of St. Mark's fondness for reproducing the very syllables that were spoken." Robertson agrees, noting the Aramaic Rabbouni, and that "the Messiah was expected to give sight to the blind" (Isaiah 61:1; Luke 4:18; 7:22), and that the man's word for sight (anablepsō, "recover sight") suggests "he had once been able to see."

The question "What wilt thou that I should do unto thee?" the expositors note, is the same Christ had asked James and John (v.36) — and the contrast of the two answers is striking. The brothers asked for thrones; the blind beggar asks only for sight. Christ draws out the man's desire, that he may name it and ask in faith.

So the witnesses make the verse the meeting of Christ's gracious question with the beggar's humble, believing petition. The Lord, who knew well what the blind man wanted, yet asks "What wilt thou?" — that the man might confess his need and his faith aloud; and Bartimaeus answers with the reverent "Rabboni" and the simple, all-comprehending request, "that I might receive my sight." The lesson the expositors draw is the directness and humility of true prayer, set against the ambition of the earlier petitioners. Where James and John, asked the same question, sought the chief places in glory, the blind man asks only for the mercy he needs — to see. His petition is at once humble and full of faith: he believes the Son of David can open his eyes, and asks for nothing more and nothing less. Mark's careful preservation of the very syllable "Rabboni," the witnesses note, gives the scene the freshness of an eyewitness memory and the warmth of the man's reverence; and his single, focused request — sight — stands as the model of prayer that knows its need and casts it wholly on Christ, who will grant it in the verse that follows with a word that joins healing to faith.
52:
"And Jesus said unto him, Go thy way; thy faith hath made thee whole. And immediately he received his sight, and followed Jesus in the way." The witnesses read the cure as the reward of faith and the beginning of discipleship. The grammatical-historical Barnes notes the alternative rendering of "made thee whole" — "or, saved thee" — joining the bodily healing to the deeper salvation; Gill paraphrases "go thy way" as "thy request is granted." Ellicott draws an inference from the close: "we may reasonably infer that Bartimaeus was one of those who went up with the travelling company to Jerusalem," and "the prominence St. Mark gives to his name suggests that he afterwards became conspicuous in the Church... his new-found sight qualifying him to take his place among the eye-witnesses of the things done in the ensuing week."

The Wesleyan Clarke draws the doctrinal reflection: "this very remarkable cure gives us another proof, not only of the sovereign power, but of the benevolence of Christ; nor do we ever see that sovereign power used but in the way of benevolence. How slow is God to punish, how prone to spare!"

So the witnesses make the verse the joyful close of the miracle and the model of true response to grace. Christ heals the beggar with a word that honours his faith — "thy faith hath made thee whole" — and the man, immediately seeing, does not go his own way but "followed Jesus in the way." The lesson the expositors draw is twofold. First, the power of faith to receive Christ's mercy: it was not the beggar's merit but his believing cry that opened his eyes, and Christ ascribes the cure to his faith. Second, the right use of restored sight: Bartimaeus, told to "go his way," chooses instead to follow Jesus, joining the company going up to Jerusalem — using his new eyes first of all to behold and follow his Healer. The contrast with the rich young man, who "went away," is complete: the beggar who had nothing, healed, follows; the rich man who had much, called, departed. And the contrast with the disciples is instructive too: while they disputed for precedence, the blind man simply followed. Bartimaeus thus becomes, in the witnesses' reading, the closing emblem of the chapter — the helpless sinner who cries to the Son of David, is heard, is healed by faith, and follows his Saviour "in the way," that very way that led up to the cross and the glory beyond it.
